title: Demonstration of a compact compressor for application of metastability-exchange optical pumping of 3He to human lung imaging
abstract:
Demonstration of a Compact Compressor for Application
of Metastability-Exchange Optical Pumping of 3He to
Human Lung Imaging
Thomas R. Gentile,1 Gordon L. Jones,1 Alan K. Thompson,1 Rahim R. Rizi,2*
David A. Roberts,2 Ivan E. Dimitrov,3 Ravinder Reddy,2 David A. Lipson,4
Warren Gefter,2 Mitchell D. Schnall,2 and John S. Leigh2
Hyperpolarized gas magnetic resonance imaging has recently
emerged as a method to image lungs, sinuses, and the brain.
The best lung images to date have been produced using hyper-
polarized 3He, which is produced by either spin-exchange or
metastability-exchange optical pumping. For hyperpolarized
gas MRI, the metastable method has demonstrated higher po-
larization levels and higher polarizing rates, but it requires com-
pression of the hyperpolarized gas. Prior to this work, compres-
sion of hyperpolarized gas had only been accomplished using a
large, complex and expensive apparatus. Here, human lung
ventilation images are presented that were obtained using a
compact compressor that is relatively simple and inexpensive.
For this test, 1.1 bar-L of 15% hyperpolarized 3He gas was
produced at the National Institute of Standards and Technology
using a modiﬁed commercial diaphragm pump. The hyperpo-
larized gas was transported to the University of Pennsylvania in
a holding ﬁeld provided by a portable solenoid.
Magn Reson
Med 43:290–294, 2000. © 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
Key words: hyperpolarized; lungs; helium; metastability; optical
pumping; compressor
Optical pumping methods for polarizing noble gases, orig-
inally developed for applications in basic and applied
physics (1,2), have recently been applied to magnetic res-
onance imaging of lungs (3–11), sinuses (12) and the brain
(13,14). In contrast with conventional MRI, which relies
on the small thermal polarization of the protons in water,
hyperpolarized gas MRI uses the large nuclear polarization
that can be produced by optical pumping. Although most
imaging to date has been done with conventional high-
ﬁeld magnets, the nonequilibrium polarization produced
by optical pumping allows for low-ﬁeld imaging with a
high signal-to-noise ratio (15,16). Hyperpolarized 3He and
129Xe have both been used for imaging, and each has its
distinct advantages: 129Xe has the capability to provide
images of organs other than the lungs because it dissolves
in blood and lipids, whereas 3He has provided excellent
images of lung and other air spaces. The nuclear magnetic
resonance (NMR) signal from 3He is eight times larger than
that from 129Xe for a given value of nuclear polarization
because it has a larger magnetic moment. In addition,
typical polarization levels have been higher for 3He, and
3He does not have the anesthetic properties of 129Xe.
Electronic and nuclear polarization can be produced in
atoms by optical pumping, which is a method that trans-
fers angular momentum from a beam of light to atoms.
Optical pumping is usually performed by illuminating
atoms with circularly polarized light at the wavelength of
the electronic transition from the ground state to the ﬁrst
excited state of the atoms, e.g., the 589-nm line for sodium.
However, direct optical pumping of 3He is not practical
because laser light at the far ultraviolet wavelength re-
quired (58 nm) is not generally available. In the last few
decades, two indirect optical pumping methods have been
developed for polarizing 3He nuclei: In the spin-exchange
method (17–20), rubidium is optically pumped, typically
using a high-power diode laser at a wavelength of 795 nm.
The resulting electronic polarization is slowly transferred
to the
3He nuclei via the hyperﬁne interaction during
collisions. In the metastability-exchange approach (21,22),
3He atoms in the 2 3S1 (metastable) atomic state are pro-
duced in a low-pressure (;1 mbar) electrical discharge
and optically pumped with a Nd:LMA (neodymium-doped
lanthanum magnesium hexaluminate) laser at a wave-
length of 1083 nm. The hyperﬁne interaction within the
metastable atom leads to polarization of its nucleus. In a
metastability-exchange collision, the incoming metastable
atom transfers its atomic excitation to the incoming
ground state atom, leaving the outgoing ground-state atom
with the polarized nucleus. Both methods have been suc-
cessfully used for human lung imaging. Compared to the
spin-exchange method, the metastable technique has dem-
onstrated higher polarization levels and rates. It has the
disadvantage, however, that it can be carried out only at
low pressures, necessitating compression of the hyperpo-
larized gas. To date, widespread use of the metastable
method for MRI applications has been hindered by the
size, cost, and complexity of the apparatus used for com-
pressing the hyperpolarized gas (23). Motivated by the
intrinsic capability of the metastable method to rapidly
produce hyperpolarized gas, the authors from NIST have
developed a compact, inexpensive, and relatively simple
apparatus to compress hyperpolarized 3He gas. (These au-
thors are developing both optical pumping methods for
neutron polarizers based on polarized 3He (24,25)). We
describe the apparatus and present human lung images
1National Institute of Standards and Technology, Gaithersburg, Maryland.
2Department of Radiology, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsyl-
vania.
3Department of Biophysics, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Penn-
sylvania.
4Division of Pulmonary, Allergy and Critical Care Medicine, University of
Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Grant sponsor: National Institutes of Health; Grant number: RR02305.
*Correspondence to: Rahim Rizi, Ph.D., Department of Radiology, MMRRCC,
University of Pennsylvania, B1 Stellar-Chance, 422 Curie Boulevard, Phila-
delphia, PA 19104-6100. E-mail: rrizi@ginger.mmrrcc.upenn.edu
Received 19 March 1999; revised 15 October 1999; accepted 19 October
1999.
Magnetic Resonance in Medicine 43:290–294 (2000)
© 2000 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
290
 generated using hyperpolarized 3He gas produced by this
apparatus. For this work, 3He gas was hyperpolarized by
optical pumping at low pressure and then compressed into
a glass storage cell. The cell was then detached from the
compression apparatus and transported to the University
of Pennsylvania in a holding magnetic ﬁeld provided by a
portable solenoid. The hyperpolarized gas was used to
obtain in vivo human lung images.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
3He Polarization and Delivery
The compressor and gas recirculation apparatus, shown in
Fig. 1, ﬁts into a cube about 30 cm on a side, and a
sufﬁciently uniform magnetic ﬁeld is provided throughout
this volume using a pair of 80-cm diameter Helmholtz
coils. The DC drive motor is located outside of the Helm-
holtz coils and connects to the compressor via a 50-cm-
long brass shaft. When the compressor is to be operated to
ﬁll a cell with polarized gas, the entire system is ﬁrst
evacuated by a turbomolecular pump with the compressor
running. Valves V1, V2, V3, and Vsg are then closed and
3He gas is admitted through a regulator (not shown) and
valve V4. The ﬂow rate is controlled by a ﬂow-restricting
capillary and a metering valve.
The 3He gas is optically pumped at low pressure using
the metastability-exchange method using an apparatus
similar to that described in Ref. 21. Metastable 3He atoms
are produced by an electrodeless rf discharge. The Nd:
LMA laser is constructed by substituting a Nd:LMA rod in
a commercial arc-lamp-pumped Nd:YAG laser, and adding
two etalons to the open laser cavity to tune the laser and
narrow its bandwidth. It produces 3 W of laser light at a
wavelength of 1083 nm. The polarization in the optical
pumping cell is monitored by measuring the degree of
circular polarization of the 668-nm light emitted by ex-
cited atoms undergoing the 3
1D2 3 2
1P1 transition
(26,27). We use methods similar to those described in Ref.
23 to preserve the high gas purity required in the optical
pumping cell: a getter is used to purify the incoming gas,
and U-shaped glass tubes are immersed in liquid nitrogen
at the inlet and outlet of the cell.
After being hyperpolarized in a 270 cm3 optical pump-
ing cell, the gas passes through a modiﬁed two-stage dia-
phragm vacuum pump and is delivered to a detachable
storage cell. Glass tubing and stopcock valves are used to
transport and direct the gas. The motorless compressor
was modiﬁed from its original form (KNF Neuberger, Inc.,
Edgebrook Park, Two Black Forest Road, Trenton, NJ)5 by
replacing all magnetic parts with nonmagnetic equiva-
lents. No modiﬁcation of the pump heads was performed
because the aluminum, Viton and Teﬂon that contact the
gas are nonmagnetic, have reasonably low outgassing
rates, and were not expected to signiﬁcantly depolarize the
gas in the relatively short time it spends in the compressor.
Hence, essentially all of the modiﬁed parts are in the drive
train of the compressor. These include a shaft, eccentric,
and counterweight that were obtained in type 316 stainless
steel by special order to the pump manufacturer. The
diaphragm studs were machined at NIST and annealed to
minimize residual magnetism before being sent to the
pump manufacturer for incorporation into the ﬁnished
diaphragms. Steel radial ballbearings were replaced with
commercially available type 316 stainless steel bearings;
for the main bearing a direct replacement was not available
so a special sleeve was fabricated. Steel retaining rings
were replaced by rings made from beryllium-copper, but
the rings themselves may not be absolutely required. In
summary, all of the modiﬁed parts were either obtained by
special order from the pump manufacturer, obtained from
other commercial sources, or within the capability of any
machine shop. Because of the economy of scale, we have
obtained sufﬁcient parts to assemble ﬁve compressors at a
total cost of under $10,000.
The polarization in the storage cell is measured using
free-induction decay NMR at a magnetic ﬁeld of 2.5 mT.
The NMR system is calibrated against the aforementioned
optical method by ﬁrst optically pumping low-pressure
5Certain trade names and company products are mentioned in the text or
identiﬁed in an illustration in order to adequately specify the experimental
procedure and equipment used. In no case does such identiﬁcation imply
recommendation or endorsement by the National Institute of Standards and
Technology, nor does it imply that the products are necessarily the best
available for the purpose.
FIG 1. Schematic diagram of the compression apparatus, including
the 3He gas bottle, glass stopcock valves (Voc, Vcs, Vs, Vsg, V1-V4),
getter (G), metering valve (MV), ﬂow restricting capillary (Cf), optical
pumping cell (OPC), liquid nitrogen traps (LN), the modiﬁed com-
pressor (two stages, St1 and St2), a capacitance manometer to
measure the optical pumping cell pressure (PL), a strain gauge
sensor to measure the storage cell pressure (PH), detachable glass
O-ring connections (D), storage cell capillaries (Cd), the storage cell
(StC), and the cell dewar. The system can be evacuated by con-
nections to a turbomolecular pump (HV).
Compressor to Produce Hyperpolarized 3He

Compressor to Produce Hyperpolarized 3He
gas in the storage cell directly, and then recording both the
optical and NMR signals from the low-pressure hyperpo-
For these experiments, the gas was accumulated in a 270
cm3 storage cell made from Corning 7056 glass (Corning
Glass, Corning, NY). This glass is known to permit longer
relaxation times than Pyrex because of its lower perme-
ability (28,29) but is easier to work with than the very
low-permeability aluminosilicate glasses such as Corning
1720. During the ﬁll of the cell with hyperpolarized gas,
the cell was immersed in liquid nitrogen to increase the
ﬁnal room temperature pressure to 4.0 bar from the nom-
inal one bar outlet pressure of the compressor. A simple
dewar was formed from a block of styrofoam and coated on
the inside with a cryogenic epoxy (Stycast 2850FT, Emer-
son and Cummings Specialty Polymers, Lexington, MA)
because the particular styrofoam that we obtained was
porous. Because the outlet pressure of the compressor rises
from zero to one bar during the ﬁll, the optical pumping
cell pressure also rises, typically from about 2 mbar to 6
mbar. The production of 1.1 bar-L of 15% hyperpolarized
gas was completed in three hours, yielding an average ﬂow
rate of 0.37 bar-L/h. After completion of the ﬁll, the storage
cell valves were closed, and the cell was detached from the
compression apparatus and placed in a solenoid to pre-
serve the polarization during the three hour car trip from
NIST in Gaithersburg, MD to the University of Pennsylva-
nia in Philadelphia, PA. The end-compensated solenoid is
60 cm long and 25 cm in diameter, and surrounded by a
single layer of magnetic shielding alloy. The magnetic ﬁeld
strength of the solenoid is 1.3 mT when powered by a 12-V
battery. The current for the solenoid was provided by a
portable battery during transport between the labs and the
car, and by the car battery otherwise. Tests showed that the
relaxation time of the hyperpolarized gas while the sole-
noid was located in the car was only slightly less than the
15-hour relaxation time of the cell itself.
At the site of the scanner, the gas was delivered to the
subject using a length of polyethylene tubing with a bal-
loon placed on a tee in the line. The subject pinched the
tubing shut. When the cell valve was opened, the gas ﬁlled
the balloon, and the subject released the tubing and in-
haled. The subject was asked to breathe in and to hold his
breath while the imaging sequence was run.
Scanning was performed on a 1.5-T whole-body imager
(Signa, GE Medical Systems, Milwaukee, WI) using a stan-
dard 1-T proton TR (transmit-receive) switch and a 10–
500-MHz, 35-dB gain, pre-ampliﬁer (MITEQ, Hauppauge,
NY). An octagonal, 26-cm diameter, transmit-receive sur-
face coil tuned to the 3He resonance frequency (48.59
MHz) was positioned over the chest of the subject. Coronal
images were obtained using a sequentially encoded 2D,
fast gradient-echo pulse sequence with 12-degree nominal
constant ﬂip angle, 18 msec/3msec TR/TE, 2563 128 ma-
trix, and one echo per excitation. Other imaging parame-
ters were: FOV (ﬁeld of view) 533 53 cm, slice thickness 9
mm, interslice spacing 1 mm, number of slices 8, and total
Figure 2 shows ventilation images of the lungs of a 25-year
old healthy male after inhalation of hyperpolarized 3He (Fig.
FIG. 2. Coronal images of the lungs of a
25-year-old healthy subject after inhala-
tion of laser hyperpolarized 3He (A–H) ,
along with a proton image of the lung of
the same individual (I), corresponding to
B. In the 3He images, the airways includ-
ing the trachea, the mainstem bronchi,
and the ventilated pulmonary paren-
chyma are hyperintense as shown by the
arrows in B and C. Arrows in G and H
show the posterior basal area of the
lungs that is outside the sensitive region
of the surface coil. The following imaging
parameters were used: matrix size 5
256 3 128, FOV 5 53 cm3 53 cm, slice
thickness 5 9 mm, TR 5 18 msec, and
2A–H), along with a proton image of the lungs of the same
individual (Fig. 2I). The proton image corresponds to the
slice in Fig. 2B. In the 3He images, the trachea and the
mainstem bronchi (large arrows), and the ventilated pulmo-
nary parenchyma are hyperintense. The images display ho-
mogenous ventilation of the hyperpolarized gas into the
lungs of the healthy subject. The lower signal intensity of the
posterior basal areas of the lungs (arrows, Fig. 2G and H) is
due to the fact that these regions were located outside the
sensitive region of the surface coil. The average signal-to-
noise ratio (SNR) was 71 for the major airways and 38 for the
lung parenchyma. The images in Fig. 2 demonstrate the tech-
nical feasibility of performing MR imaging using hyperpolar-
ized 3He produced by the new compact compressor.
We have demonstrated the use of a compact compressor
for hyperpolarized 3He gas to produce high-quality images
of pulmonary ventilation. The emerging ﬁeld of hyperpo-
larized gas imaging depends upon convenient, rapid, and
inexpensive methods to produce hyperpolarized gas.
Goals for the future include improving the polarization
level and production rate, making the apparatus more
simple, robust, and compact, and performing further im-
For this compressor, the achievable polarization using
pure 3He is limited by the relatively high pressure in the
optical pumping cell, which ideally would be about one
mbar. The contribution to the optical pumping cell pres-
sure due to gas ﬂow could be reduced by using two com-
pressor heads in parallel for the ﬁrst stage. Using a third
compression stage would increase the overall compression
ratio. Since almost one-third of the polarization is lost in
compression, reducing the polarization loss per stage is
critical to the success of a three-stage system.
We are presently investigating the variation of the achiev-
able polarization with gas ﬂow rate. At higher ﬂow rates, the
polarization in the optical pumping cell decreases for two
reasons: the inlet pressure increases and the gas has less time
to be hyperpolarized. To address the latter issue, we are
experimenting with two larger optical pumping cells, but
without violating the compact spirit of the apparatus. We
have decreased the ﬁll time for one bar-L of gas to less than
two hours without signiﬁcant loss of polarization, and expect
that further improvement in the polarizing rate will be forth-
coming. In all of the hyperpolarized gas production methods,
there will be a trade-off of polarization and production rate,
and the desired image resolution may dictate which param-
eter is of greater importance.
To sidestep the limitations of the high inlet pressure in the
present apparatus, we have employed optical pumping of
3He-4He mixtures (30) for our work on neutron polarizers.
For a given total pressure, this approach permits higher 3He
polarization to be obtained. We have obtained 3He polariza-
tion as high as 46% at a total pressure of 2.0 bar using a gas
mixture containing 25% 3He and 75% 4He (24). This ap-
proach is not currently desirable for hyperpolarized gas MRI
because only the 3He has nuclear polarization, so the total
number of hyperpolarized nuclei delivered is less than what
can be obtained with pure 3He. However, if the spin density
is high enough, it may provide a means for reducing the
amount of costly 3He used for an image.
Although the arc-lamp pumped Nd:LMA laser is cur-
rently the best choice, the power supply is relatively large
and requires water cooling and three-phase power. Re-
cently an all-solid-state laser option has emerged: a Yb-
doped ﬁber ampliﬁer coupled with a low-power diode
laser oscillator (31). Such systems are slowly becoming
commercially available, and we expect further improve-
ments and availability in the near future.
1. Chupp TE, Holt RJ, Milner RG. Optically pumped polarized H, D, and
3He gas targets. Annu Rev Nucl Part Sci 1994;45:373–411.
2. Leduc, M. In: Boudard A, Terrien Y, editors. Spin-polarized helium-3,
a playground in many domains of physics. J Phys (Paris) Colloq 1990:
3. deLange EE, Mugler III JP, Brookeman JR, Knight-Scott J, Truwit, J,
Teates CD, Daniel TM, Bogorad PL, Cates GD. Lung airspaces: MR
